prompt_md: A base class has a `protected` member `int score`. Which of the following can access `score` directly?

options.a: Code in `main()` via an object of the base class
options.b: Code in a publicly derived class's member function
options.c: Code in `main()` via an object of the derived class
options.d: Only the base class itself â€” protected is equivalent to private

explanation_md: `protected` members are accessible inside any derived class's member functions (option b), but not from outside the class hierarchy (`main()` cannot access them directly through any object). Option d is wrong â€” `protected` is specifically designed to allow derived class access.

`B::show()` accesses `x` (public, inherited) and `y` (protected, accessible inside derived). It prints `1 2`. In `main()`, `b.x` is accessible because it is public â€” prints `1`. `b.y` and `b.z` would be errors outside the class hierarchy.

options.a: `Circle` uses public inheritance but should use protected inheritance to access `area`
options.b: `area` is `private` in `Shape` â€” derived classes cannot access private members regardless of inheritance specifier
options.c: `int area = 0` is invalid syntax inside a class without a constructor
options.d: The inheritance specifier is missing â€” writing `: Shape` without a keyword is required for access

explanation_md: `private` members of a base class are inaccessible in any derived class, regardless of whether inheritance is `public`, `protected`, or `private`. To let `Circle` access `area`, the base must declare it `protected` instead of `private`.

prompt_md: Class `B` uses `private` inheritance from class `A` (`class B : private A`). `A` has a `public` method `void doWork()`. From outside both classes (e.g. in `main()`), can you call `doWork()` on a `B` object?

options.a: Yes â€” `public` methods of A are always accessible through any derived object
options.b: No â€” `private` inheritance makes `doWork()` private in `B`, so it is inaccessible outside `B`
options.c: Yes â€” but only if `B` explicitly re-declares `doWork()` as public
options.d: Only if `B` uses `using A::doWork` to restore the access level

explanation_md: Private inheritance makes all public and protected members of the base become `private` in the derived class. `doWork()` becomes private in `B`, so it cannot be called from `main()`. Option d (using `using A::doWork`) could restore it, but the question asks about the default behavior.
